question: Explain the steps involved in mapping a weak entity type to relational schema with an example.
expectedPoints: Identify the owner entity and weak entity
Include all attributes of weak entity in new relation
Add the primary key of owner entity as foreign key
The combination of owner PK and weak entity's partial key forms composite primary key
Example: Payment (weak) owned by Account (regular) - AccountNumber becomes FK in Payment

question: How is a binary M:N relationship mapped to relational schema? Explain with example.
expectedPoints: Create a new relation for the relationship
Include primary keys of both participating entities as foreign keys
These foreign keys together form the composite primary key
Include any attributes of the relationship
Example: Student and Course with Enroll relationship - creates Enroll(StudentID, CourseID, Grade)

question: Differentiate between entity integrity and referential integrity with examples.
expectedPoints: Entity Integrity: Primary key cannot be NULL - every entity must have unique identifier
Referential Integrity: Foreign key must reference existing tuple or be NULL
Example: Student(SID, Name) - SID cannot be NULL (entity integrity)
Enroll(SID, CID) - SID must exist in Student or be NULL (referential integrity)
Violations create orphan records

question: How are multivalued and derived attributes handled in ER-to-Relational mapping?
expectedPoints: Multivalued: Create separate relation with entity's PK as FK and the attribute
Derived: Do NOT store - compute at runtime from other stored attributes
Example: Employee with PhoneNumbers(multivalued) and Age(derived from DOB)
Employee(EmpID, Name, DOB) and Phone(EmpID, PhoneNumber)
Age is calculated when needed from DOB and current date

question: Explain mapping of ternary relationships with an example.
expectedPoints: Ternary relationship involves 3 entity types
Create new relation including primary keys of all 3 entities as foreign keys
The combination of all 3 foreign keys forms the composite primary key
Include any relationship attributes
Example: Project with Employee and Department - Creates ProjectAssign(ProjectID, EmpID, DeptID, Role)

question: How are recursive (unary) relationships mapped? Explain for both 1:1 and 1:N cases.
expectedPoints: Recursive relationships involve same entity type on both sides
For 1:1 and 1:N: Add foreign key to the same entity relation referencing its own PK
For M:N: Create separate relation with two foreign keys to the same entity
Example: Employee supervises Employee - Employee table has ManagerID FK referencing EmpID
M:N case: Needs separate relation like WorksWith(EmployeeID1, EmployeeID2)

question: Describe mapping of composite attributes in ER-to-Relational transformation.
expectedPoints: Composite attributes have multiple component attributes
Each simple component is included as separate attribute in relation
Composite attribute name is replaced by its components
Example: Address(Street, City, State, ZipCode) stored as separate columns
The logical structure remains while physical implementation flattens the composite

question: Consider an ER diagram for a Library Management System with entities: Member(MemberID, Name, Address, DateOfJoin), Book(BookID, Title, Author, ISBN), Publisher(PublisherID, Name, Address). Relationships: Member borrows Book (with attributes BorrowDate, ReturnDate) - a Member can borrow many Books, a Book can be borrowed by many Members. Publisher publishes Book - one Publisher can publish many Books, one Book has one Publisher. Draw relational schemas after mapping and specify primary keys, foreign keys, and integrity constraints.
expectedPoints: Member(MemberID(PK), Name, Address, DateOfJoin)
Book(BookID(PK), Title, Author, ISBN, PublisherID(FK))
Publisher(PublisherID(PK), Name, Address)
Borrow(MemberID(FK), BookID(FK), BorrowDate, ReturnDate, PRIMARY KEY(MemberID, BookID))
Referential integrity: PublisherID references Publisher, MemberID references Member, BookID references Book
MemberID and BookID in Borrow are foreign keys forming composite PK
Additional constraints: ReturnDate > BorrowDate, etc.

question: Map the following ER diagram to relational schema: Entities: Department(DeptID, Name, Location), Employee(EmpID, Name, Salary, DateOfBirth). Relationships: Department manages Employee (1:N), Employee works on Project with attribute Hours (M:N), Employee reports to Employee (recursive 1:N for hierarchy). Provide complete relational schema with keys and constraints.
expectedPoints: Department(DeptID(PK), Name, Location)
Employee(EmpID(PK), Name, Salary, DateOfBirth, DeptID(FK), ManagerID(FK))
Project(ProjectID(PK), Name, etc.)
WorksOn(EmpID(FK), ProjectID(FK), Hours, PRIMARY KEY(EmpID, ProjectID))
Constraints: DeptID references Department, ManagerID references Employee (self-join)
For recursive: Employee has ManagerID as FK to Employee.EmpID
WorksOn represents M:N between Employee and Project with Hours attribute

question: A University has Entities: Student(StudentID, Name, DOB), Course(CourseID, Title, Credits), Instructor(InstructorID, Name, Specialization). Relationships: Student enrols in Course (M:N with Grade), Instructor teaches Course (1:N or 1:1), Instructor advises Student (1:N). Map this ER diagram to relational schema, clearly indicating primary keys, foreign keys, and integrity constraints.
expectedPoints: Student(StudentID(PK), Name, DOB, AdvisorID(FK))
Course(CourseID(PK), Title, Credits, InstructorID(FK))
Instructor(InstructorID(PK), Name, Specialization)
Enroll(StudentID(FK), CourseID(FK), Grade, PRIMARY KEY(StudentID, CourseID))
Referential integrity: AdvisorID references Instructor, InstructorID in Course references Instructor
Student to Instructor (advising): FK in Student
Course to Instructor (teaches): FK in Course
Enroll is intersection table for M:N with Grade attribute

question: Explain the complete process of ER-to-Relational mapping with all mapping rules. Include: (i) Regular and weak entities, (ii) All types of attributes, (iii) Binary relationships with different cardinalities, (iv) N-ary relationships, (v) Integrity constraints.
expectedPoints: Step 1: Map regular entities - create relation with all simple attributes, PK is entity's identifier
Step 2: Map weak entities - add owner PK as FK, composite PK with partial key
Step 3: Map attributes - simple: direct; composite: flatten; multivalued: separate; derived: skip
Step 4: Map binary relationships - 1:1: choose side for FK; 1:N: FK in N side; M:N: intersection table
Step 5: Map N-ary: create relation with all participating entity PKs as FKs forming composite PK
Step 6: Map unary - add FK to same table or create intersection table
Step 7: Specify integrity - entity integrity (PK not NULL), referential integrity (FK valid or NULL), participation constraints (NOT NULL for total participation)
